text: Alberto Di Maria is an AI & Audio Engineer based in Italy, working as a freelance AI developer. He builds AI voice agents that handle real conversations end-to-end — from speech-to-text, through an LLM with tool-calling, to streaming text-to-speech — and ships the full product around them (frontend, backend, payments, observability).

His background bridges audio and machine learning. He holds a Master's degree (thesis on benchmarking generative music models) and has worked on audio software and signal processing, including a period as an Audio Developer at Anecoica Studio and a workshop with the Music Technology Group (MTG) in Barcelona.

Core strengths: real-time voice pipelines (barge-in, low latency, turn-taking), agentic tool-calling, retrieval-augmented generation, full-stack web (Next.js, Supabase, Stripe), and the engineering rigor that proves a system works — evaluation suites, latency metrics (p50/p95), tests, CI and observability.

He is the kind of engineer who measures what he builds: not "it seems to work" but eval scores, success rates and latency percentiles. He cares about cost-consciousness, security hygiene, and shipping things that are genuinely production-ready.
Contact: dimaria.alberto@outlook.com. Portfolio: albedimaria.github.io.

Beat Store is a full-stack beat marketplace Alberto built and shipped to production — a real e-commerce product where producers sell beats with tiered licenses. It is the full-stack counterpart to his AI work: it proves he can ship an entire product, not just a model.

Stack: Next.js, TypeScript, Supabase, Stripe for payments, deployed on Netlify, with PostHog analytics and Google login. Features include a cart with bundle promotions (buy 3 / 5 / 8), license tiers, an admin dashboard, and producer management.

The standout is the hardening pass for production rigor. Starting from a codebase with no tests and no CI, Alberto added: 34 money-path tests (Vitest unit tests plus Playwright end-to-end), reaching about 86% statement coverage and 100% function coverage on the money path — checkout, Stripe webhook signature and idempotency, bundle pricing, license PDF contracts. He added a GitHub Actions CI pipeline (typecheck, lint, test, e2e) that gates every push, and Sentry for error tracking and p95 latency.

Security was already solid before hardening: Stripe webhook signature verification and idempotency, server-side pricing (never trusting client amounts). The hardening was about proving correctness with tests and measuring it with observability — turning "ships on vibes" into "ships with evidence".
Company Brain is a tool-calling business agent Alberto built at an Italian hackathon, where it placed in the Top 10 out of around 100 builders — qualifying for the national Hackathon League.

The idea: a single agent that acts as the "brain" of a company, answering questions and taking actions across the business by calling tools (querying data, running operations) rather than just chatting. It showcases agentic tool-calling applied to a real business context — the agent decides which tool to invoke to satisfy a request, chains calls, and returns grounded answers.

It sits in the same family as Alberto's other agentic work (Shy Order's multi-agent orchestration, Dance Voice Agent's parallel tool-calling): the throughline is an LLM that doesn't just talk but acts, with the orchestration logic and tool contracts engineered deliberately rather than left to chance.

Dance Voice Agent is Alberto's flagship voice project: a real-time phone voice agent for a dance school that handles real conversations entirely over the phone. It identifies students, books classes, schedules makeup sessions, and handles trial-lesson requests.

Architecture — a real-time voice pipeline with barge-in: Deepgram for speech-to-text, GPT-4o with parallel tool-calling as the brain, and ElevenLabs streaming text-to-speech for the voice, in Italian and Spanish. The barge-in (letting the caller interrupt the agent) is triggered by the first interim transcript, not by a separate voice-activity event.
Stack: Python, FastAPI, Twilio (telephony), Supabase (data), Next.js admin dashboard, deployed on Render and Vercel.

What makes it a flagship is the engineering rigor. Alberto built an Eval & Observability layer: an evaluation suite that runs fixed scenarios against the real LLM and database, scoring task success (6/6 scenarios passing) and measuring latency — p50 around 4.2 seconds and p95 around 6.3 seconds, end to end. Per-turn latency is instrumented (time-to-first-token, TTS time-to-first-byte, tool time, total response) and persisted, with an admin dashboard showing call analytics, booking trends, latency and containment metrics.
The repository is public (anonymized) and includes a STUDY_GUIDE documenting the architecture, latency budget and design decisions.

Lyra is a lyrics-first music discovery experience, built at the Musixmatch hackathon (the "Musicathon") with a teammate. Instead of recommending tracks by audio features alone, Lyra centers the lyrics: it surfaces music through what the words say and the emotion they carry, and for each track it cites one specific lyric line that matches.

The interface is a wheel of emotions with curved, clickable labels rendered as SVG textPaths along arcs. Under it sits an emotion-buffer model: a FIFO of the last few moods, weighted so a repeated mood counts double, with a positional weighting that gives the first pick the strongest pull. The playlist starts once a few moods are selected and updates as the user spins the wheel.

Stack: Next.js 16, TypeScript, react-three-fiber for the 3D/interactive layer, with an Anthropic Claude agent on the backend and a Musixmatch recommendation backend, deployed on Vercel with the backend on Hugging Face Spaces.
Lyra is Alberto's most creative, design-forward project — where audio, ML and a crafted interactive front end meet. It's also where he first worked seriously with react-three-fiber.

Shy Order is a multi-agent system that calls restaurants on the user's behalf to book a table — for people who would rather not make the phone call themselves. The user states what they want; the agent finds the restaurant, places the call, talks to the staff, and reports back.

It runs on ElevenLabs Conversational AI for the voice side. Restaurant discovery uses LLM-based extraction: instead of brittle regex/HTML scraping, an LLM with a forced tool call pulls the phone number and details from the restaurant's web presence. A concrete bug Alberto found and fixed: phone numbers that lived only inside a `tel:` link (not in visible text) were being missed, so he passed the `tel:` hrefs to the LLM as hints.
Stack: Python, FastAPI, Twilio, Supabase, with an admin dashboard. The system separates the conversation into implicit phases (talking to the user vs talking to the restaurant).

Shy Order demonstrates multi-agent orchestration and robust real-world tool use — the messy reality of getting structured data (a working phone number) out of unstructured web pages, and driving a real phone conversation to a goal.

Alberto's Master's thesis (Metatron) is an empirical, hands-on benchmark of generative music models. Rather than reporting benchmark numbers from papers, he evaluated a range of generative models himself — exploring text-to-music and audio generation systems such as MusicGen, Stable Audio, RAVE and MusicVAE, and using audio embeddings (Essentia, TensorFlow) for analysis.

He evaluated around thirteen generative models hands-on, under real hardware constraints (ROCm/CUDA), documenting what each could and couldn't do in practice — including how hard certain explorations (like VAE latent spaces) turned out to be. The framing is empirical evaluation from direct use, not benchmark-table citation.

The thesis sits under Education with a research slant, and it is single-authored: Alberto did the work from scratch. It connects his audio background to the generative-AI wave, and it's the project that most directly shows research-style rigor — designing an evaluation, running it, and reporting honest findings about model capabilities and limits.
